title: Min Stack
questionBody:
Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving the minimum element in constant time.

Implement the MinStack class:

  • MinStack() initializes the stack object.
  • void push(int val) pushes the element val onto the stack.
  • void pop() removes the element on the top of the stack.
  • int top() gets the top element of the stack.
  • int getMin() retrieves the minimum element in the stack.

You must implement a solution with O(1) time complexity for each function.

 

Example 1:

Input
["MinStack","push","push","push","getMin","pop","top","getMin"]
[[],[-2],[0],[-3],[],[],[],[]]

Output
[null,null,null,null,-3,null,0,-2]

Explanation
MinStack minStack = new MinStack();
minStack.push(-2);
minStack.push(0);
minStack.push(-3);
minStack.getMin(); // return -3
minStack.pop();
minStack.top();    // return 0
minStack.getMin(); // return -2

 

Constraints:

  • -231 <= val <= 231 - 1
  • Methods pop, top and getMin operations will always be called on non-empty stacks.
  • At most 3 * 104 calls will be made to push, pop, top, and getMin.
clarifyingQuestions: Are there any constraints on the range of integer values that can be pushed onto the stack?
What should `getMin()` return if the stack is empty?
Can the stack store duplicate values, and how should duplicates be handled when retrieving the minimum?
What is the expected behavior of `pop()` or `top()` when the stack is empty?
Are there any constraints on the total number of operations (push, pop, top, getMin) that will be performed on the stack?

edgeCases.edgeCase: Calling pop, top, or getMin on an empty stack
edgeCases.handling: The implementation should raise an error or return a sentinel value (e.g., None or an exception) to indicate an invalid operation.
The solution must update the stored minimum to the new value, ensuring getMin() always reflects the current minimum.
The solution needs to correctly track the previous minimum value to maintain accurate getMin() results.
Pushing and then immediately popping or getting the minimum should function correctly.
The minimum should remain constant, and pop operations should correctly update to the same previous minimum.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Stack with negative numbers and zero
edgeCases.handling: The comparison logic for minimums must handle negative values and zero correctly.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Extremely large number of pushes leading to memory limits
edgeCases.handling: The solution's space complexity should be considered; using an auxiliary stack for minimums is generally efficient.

edgeCases.edgeCase: Pushing values that approach integer limits
edgeCases.handling: Ensure the chosen data type for storing stack elements and minimums can accommodate the full range of potential input values without overflow.

bruteForce.overview: The brute force approach to this problem involves checking every single number that has been added to our special stack to find the minimum value. We do this every time we need to know what the minimum is.
steps: When we want to add a new number, we just put it on top of our existing numbers.
When we want to find the smallest number, we look at all the numbers currently in our stack, one by one.
We keep track of the smallest number we've seen so far as we look at each number.
Once we have examined every number, the one we kept track of as the smallest is our answer.
When we want to remove the top number, we simply take it off the top of our stack.

bigO.runtimeExplanation: When adding an element, it's a constant time operation, O(1). When removing an element, it's also a constant time operation, O(1). The critical operation is finding the minimum. Since we iterate through all 'n' elements currently in the stack to find the minimum, this operation takes O(n) time. As finding the minimum can be called frequently, the overall time complexity for the getMin operation is O(n). Thus, the dominant factor is the getMin operation.
bigO.spaceExplanation: The provided brute force approach to the Min Stack problem utilizes a single stack data structure to store the elements. When finding the minimum, it iterates through the existing elements but does not allocate any new data structures that grow with the number of elements (N). The only auxiliary space used is for a few variables to keep track of the minimum value during iteration, which is constant. Therefore, the auxiliary space complexity is O(1).

code.Kotlin: import java.util.ArrayList;import java.util.List;import java.util.Objects;class MinStackBruteForce {    private List<Integer> stackElements;    public MinStackBruteForce() {        stackElements = new ArrayList<>();    }    public void push(int value) {        stackElements.add(value);    }    public void pop() {        if (!stackElements.isEmpty()) {            stackElements.remove(stackElements.size() - 1);        }    }    public int top() {        if (!stackElements.isEmpty()) {            return stackElements.get(stackElements.size() - 1);        }        // This check is crucial to handle cases where the stack is empty        throw new IllegalStateException("Stack is empty");    }    public int getMin() {        // We need to iterate through all elements to find the minimum        if (stackElements.isEmpty()) {            // Returning an indicator of an empty stack is necessary            throw new IllegalStateException("Stack is empty");        }        int currentMinimum = stackElements.get(0);        // This loop ensures we examine every element to find the true minimum        for (int element : stackElements) {            if (element < currentMinimum) {                currentMinimum = element;            }        }        // The smallest element found after checking all others is the result        return currentMinimum;    }}
code.Ruby:
class MinStack
  def initialize
    @stack_elements = []
  end

  def push(value)
    @stack_elements.push(value)
  end

  def pop
    @stack_elements.pop
  end

  def top
    @stack_elements.last
  end

  def get_min
    # We need to iterate through all elements to find the smallest one
    if @stack_elements.empty?
      return nil
    end

    current_minimum = @stack_elements.first

    # Check each element to see if it's smaller than our current minimum
    @stack_elements.each do |element|
      if element < current_minimum
        current_minimum = element
      end
    end

    # The element tracked as smallest is the overall minimum
    current_minimum
  end
end
code.PHP:
<?php

class MinStack {
    private array $stackElements;

    function __construct() {
        $this->stackElements = [];
    }

    function push(int $value): void {
        // Add the new element to the top of the stack
        $this->stackElements[] = $value;
    }

    function pop(): void {
        // Remove the top element from the stack
        if (!empty($this->stackElements)) {
            array_pop($this->stackElements);
        }
    }

    function top(): int {
        // Return the top element without removing it
        if (!empty($this->stackElements)) {
            return $this->stackElements[count($this->stackElements) - 1];
        }
        // Return a sentinel value or throw an exception if stack is empty
        return -1; // Placeholder for empty stack
    }

    function getMin(): int {
        // Find the minimum value by iterating through all elements
        if (empty($this->stackElements)) {
            return -1; // Placeholder for empty stack
        }

        $currentMinimumValue = $this->stackElements[0];

        // Iterate through all numbers to find the smallest one
        foreach ($this->stackElements as $element) {
            if ($element < $currentMinimumValue) {
                $currentMinimumValue = $element;
            }
        }
        // The smallest value found is returned
        return $currentMinimumValue;
    }
}

?>

optimal.overview: The goal is to create a special kind of stack that can quickly tell you the smallest number currently inside it. The trick is to keep track of not just the numbers themselves, but also what the smallest number was when each number was added.
steps: Imagine you have two separate storage areas. One is for the numbers you are actively putting into your stack.
The other storage area is specifically for keeping track of the smallest number seen so far at each step.

When you add a new number to the main storage, you also look at the current smallest number recorded. You then add either the new number itself, or the previous smallest number, whichever is smaller, to the smallest-tracker storage.
This way, the top of the smallest-tracker storage always holds the smallest number that is currently in the main storage.
When you remove a number from the main storage, you also remove the corresponding smallest number from the smallest-tracker storage.
To find the minimum, you simply look at the very top of the smallest-tracker storage.

bigO.runtimeExplanation: The approach uses two stacks, one for the actual data and one to track the minimum element at each step. When pushing an element, we push the new element onto the data stack and the minimum of the new element and the current minimum onto the min-tracking stack. When popping, we pop from both stacks. When getting the minimum, we simply peek at the top of the min-tracking stack. All these operations involve a constant number of stack operations, regardless of the number of elements (n) currently in the stack. Therefore, each operation takes constant time.
bigO.spaceExplanation: The solution uses two storage areas: one for the main numbers and another to track the minimum at each step.  Both of these storage areas will store an entry for each element pushed onto the stack. If 'n' is the number of elements in the stack, the auxiliary space required by these two storage areas grows linearly with 'n'. Therefore, the space complexity is O(n).

code.PHP:
<?php

class MinStack {
    private array $mainStack;
    private array $minTrackingStack;

    function __construct() {
        $this->mainStack = [];
        $this->minTrackingStack = [];
    }

    function push(int $value): void {
        array_push($this->mainStack, $value);

        // Determine the minimum value to track for this push operation.
        if (empty($this->minTrackingStack)) {
            array_push($this->minTrackingStack, $value);
        } else {
            $currentMinimum = $this->minTrackingStack[count($this->minTrackingStack) - 1];
            // Store the smaller of the new value and the current minimum to maintain the minimum at each level.
            array_push($this->minTrackingStack, min($value, $currentMinimum));
        }
    }

    function pop(): void {
        if (!empty($this->mainStack)) {
            array_pop($this->mainStack);
            // When an element is popped, its corresponding minimum tracker must also be removed.
            array_pop($this->minTrackingStack);
        }
    }

    function top(): int {
        // Return the top element of the main stack.
        if (!empty($this->mainStack)) {
            return $this->mainStack[count($this->mainStack) - 1];
        }
        return -1; // Or throw an exception for empty stack
    }

    function getMin(): int {
        // The minimum value is always at the top of the min tracking stack.
        if (!empty($this->minTrackingStack)) {
            return $this->minTrackingStack[count($this->minTrackingStack) - 1];
        }
        return -1; // Or throw an exception for empty stack
    }
}

?>
